data.title: A Legendary Investor Is Gone - But His Strategy Might Matter More Than Ever
data.description:
Introduction

But I'll say this, as most of you probably know, I spent decades trying to convince people to be long-term investors, and I haven't convinced one person yet. They all want to trade. - Murray Stahl (August 19, 2025), via UnhedgedChatter.

I have often said that the stock market has become incredibly tricky. In fact, it’s the main reason why I do the work that I do, as we are dealing with so many factors that have turned what used to be rather straightforward business cycles into complex puzzles.

Inflation is still a problem, geopolitical tensions have been sky-high for years, including multiple wars that have all impacted the market and economic numbers, we’re dealing with unprecedented disruption risks from AI, and countless other things that are all connected in some way.

Dealing with all of this requires a solid strategy and an even stronger mindset. A big part of my investment journey was following Murray Stahl. What Warren Buffett is to many is what Murray Stahl was to me, a very successful investor I admired for his approach.

As you may have noticed, I used the past tense here, as Murray Stahl sadly and unexpectedly passed away on April 7. According to the Seeking Alpha news article:

Horizon Kinetics (HKHC) on Wednesday announced with deep sorrow that Murray Stahl, the company's chairman, and chief executive officer, passed away suddenly on April 7, 2026.

Murray co-founded Horizon Kinetics in 1994. He was instrumental in transforming it into a publicly traded company with a market capitalization of approximately $600M, an impeccable and debt-free balance sheet, and a portfolio built for the long horizon. Murray's dedication, wisdom, and leadership will be felt for generations to come, the company said. - Seeking Alpha.

If you are familiar with my work, you know that I have used various aspects of his research and approach in my work, including the Equity Yield Curve, which I covered in an in-depth article last year. This model is based on the theory that short-term-focused markets offer long-term opportunities because most investors are focused on chasing short-term performance for various reasons, while the financial media also tends to care more about catchy headlines about current developments than “boring” long-term theses.

Needless to say, I am painting with a broad brush, but that’s the gist of it.

Moreover, we had some things in common, including our love for landowners and stock exchanges, which are both “toll-booth-like” businesses. This includes Texas Pacific Land (TPL), a stock I bought before I was familiar with Murray Stahl and LandBridge (LB), a stock I bought the day after its IPO when I heard Stahl mention it in an investor round table.

Both stocks sold off aggressively, with Texas Pacific Land falling more than 15.0%.

This decline created one of my worst days in years from a financial point of view, as the market seems to be afraid that with Murray Stahl no longer being a Board member of Texas Pacific, the company may not be so focused on shareholder value anymore. As I will explain in future articles (this is not a TPL article), I do not believe that will be the case.

We need to be aware that Murray Stahl built a strong team over the years, including people like Steven Bregman, Peter Doyle, Tom Ewing, and research analysts like James Davolos, with whom I agree on many things, including how to build an inflation-resistant portfolio.

In other words, when I praise Murray Stahl in this article, we always need to view it in the context of the bigger team, as we can assume major investment decisions were made in a team setting, so to speak.

With that said, this article is all about what I learned, how this can help us build portfolios, and why I admired this approach so much, especially for an environment like the one we’re currently in.

Even if you have never heard of Mr. Stahl, I believe you’ll learn a lot today. At least, that’s the goal.

So, let’s dive in!

The Realization That We’re In A “New Normal”

At this point, I need to mention that I didn’t just copy and paste Murray Stahl. What I liked about him is that he had done so much work on things that I agree with as well, including our love for specific business models. For example, I was long CME Group (CME) way before I found out about how Horizon Kinetics used stock exchanges.

And, even more important, I have no ties with Horizon Kinetics in any way. That’s crucial for me to mention, as I am saying a lot of good things about a company that offers its own investment services and ETFs. As much as I would have liked to meet Mr. Stahl and his team, I doubt they know who I am.

Anyway, what I liked so much is how they always include the Big Picture in their approach. For example, for more than 30 years, the market has been a rather good place to be, even if it has had a number of steep selloffs. This includes a long-term decline in real interest rates, mostly favorable inflation tailwinds, consistently rising corporate profits, and long-term productivity benefits (think of the computer and the Internet going mainstream, wireless connectivity, and AI, among other things).

Also, government finances were way more favorable. For a few years, we’ve been dealing with consistently elevated deficits and a debt-to-GDP ratio in the triple-digit range. I don’t want to take too many detours, but just to give you another example of why this matters, the elevated deficit and debt load make surges in interest rates an even bigger risk.

It all adds to macro and market complexities that I discuss almost on a daily basis.

Now, the situation is way more complex. Although I am not bearish on the market, my view is that we are likely to see subdued returns in the years ahead, as inflation is now consistently above the Fed’s target, geopolitical risks are elevated, supply chains are suffering from deglobalization, as economic re-shoring is now more important than chasing high margins (from a nation’s point-of-view), energy supply growth is less rapid, while demand is booming, and so many other factors are playing a role.

That’s why I have been a big believer in the “run it hot” thesis, which is based on my belief (supported by our intel) that the government will accept higher inflation if it leads to accelerating and more broad-based nominal growth. So far, that seems to be the case.

However, it also means that the portfolio constructions that worked so well since the Great Financial Crisis may not work so well anymore going forward.

A New Approach For Long-Term Wealth

If there’s one thing I often say, it’s that I’m a long-term investor. I do not care about short-term developments, and I always focus on assets that can generate wealth for many years to come, even if I have a specific thesis in mind.

I want to create durable portfolios instead of creating a scenario where we all end up owning “event-driven” portfolios. For some, that may work, but for most, that’s not the best way.

What Murray Stahl understood way before I knew how to spell “stock market” is that these long-term compounders have very specific characteristics. It’s why he did not invest in traditional oil and gas companies (at least, they were not among his holdings when he passed).

Murray Stahl (and his team) cared a lot about capital expenditures and inflation. For example, as bullish as I may be on some oil and gas producers (I own Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ)), these companies require a lot of capital to operate. They invest in machinery, materials, labor, and so many other things. The same goes for mining companies. When commodity prices rise, they benefit. However, they also deal with input inflation that can pressure margins.

Instead, they went for “capital light” companies that are part of “fully-cycle” real estate exposure. It consists of companies with real assets that focus on areas with limited supply, capital-light business models that allow for compounding shareholder wealth over time, and attractive valuations that are based on more than favorable P/E ratios.

Essentially, they want to own “the house.” They called them “croupiers” in their Q4 '25 letter (and in various research pieces before that). Think of a company like CME Group (CME). This company is the largest futures exchange operator on the planet. Every time someone trades major futures contracts on WTI crude oil, NYMEX natural gas, copper, gold, silver, the S&P 500, and other assets, CME makes money. This business model is easy to scale, as trading is done electronically. Also, launching new products is often based on licenses and regulatory filings. It doesn’t require CME to build a factory or a new office.

And then there’s Texas Pacific Land, which owns almost 900 thousand surface acres in the Permian Basin. It has surface, water, and mineral rights, which means that it makes money on everything that happens on its land and under the ground. Oil companies pay royalties in exchange for drilling rights, while others pay for the right to pump produced water into the ground. It’s also working with hyperscalers to bring data centers to its land and provide the power for them.

All of this happens with close to nothing in CapEx, as oil companies are responsible for their own expenses. This provides double inflation protection.

Just like a real croupier in a casino, they take a cut without taking on the risk.

I truly wish I had come up with that, as the framework shows a powerful “flywheel effect.”

There’s one thing I did come up with, though.

And, on a side note, while I tend to follow the croupier model, I still invested in Canadian Natural Resources, as I already briefly mentioned. This company may be an oil producer, yet it owns oil sand operations in Canada. These are expensive operations, yet most costs are upfront, as the mining infrastructure needs to be built. Once that’s taken care of, they enjoy very low operating costs and massive reserves without decline rates.

It’s the croupier mindset applied in a different setting.

Focusing On The Equity Yield Curve

My personal strategy is focused on taking concentrated positions in high-conviction ideas that have a clear strategic advantage, including energy (landowners, mostly), defense, logistics, and construction suppliers (not asset-heavy construction companies).

And, just like Murray Stahl and his team, I focus on long-term opportunities that are somewhat hidden. Last year was a great example of that. While the market, in general, was focused on AI, I bought unloved energy stocks and cyclical value, as I expected an upswing in leading growth indicators.

While I make mistakes, that wasn’t a mistake, as this year, so far, has seen outperformance of cyclical value, energy (the S&P 500 leader), and an upswing in economic growth expectations (i.e., the ISM Manufacturing Index).

I did the same in 2020/2021. We want to buy high-quality businesses when they are unloved, simply because the market is occupied with something else. On the flip side, we also avoided software due to disruption risks. It took a while for that thesis to unfold, yet AI was the main factor that showed how rapidly a competitive moat can evaporate.

When combining this investment approach with the croupier/asset-light framework, we can create ultra-resilient portfolios that are not only perfect for what I expect to be a lasting change in the investment environment, but also protect us in case we’re wrong, as most companies that I own also did well over the past ten years. It will just make it harder for me to outperform. But in general, I love the risk/reward, and the chart below shows why.

Moreover, I expanded a bit on this model, as I built the TOLL model, something I am increasingly using in my regular articles. I introduced this concept in an article titled “Forget FANG, I’m Betting On ‘TOLL’ Stocks Instead.”

It is very similar to the CapEx-light model, except that it makes exceptions. And, to be fair, it was initially based on the HALO model that was introduced by Ritholz CEO Josh Brown.

This model is based on finding companies with tangible assets (the T) that have limited competition (the O of oligopoly), low-incremental capital intensity (the first L), and long-duration cash flows (the second L). For example, technically speaking, the croupier model does not favor railroads or midstream companies, as these require elevated capital to build rail and pipeline networks. That’s one of the reasons why the midstream industry, in general, struggled so much to generate shareholder value before the pandemic, as CapEx requirements were simply overwhelming.

Think of Enterprise Products Partners (EPD). Although it has one of the best track records in its industry, once the shale revolution gained momentum and massive CapEx for infrastructure was needed, its stock wasn’t popular.

However, once these companies have their assets in place, they start to generate income with limited incremental CapEx. That’s favorable for margins and free cash flow, and one of the main reasons why I came up with the TOLL model.

It’s also why I stick to what I wrote in my first TOLL article:

  • I invest in cyclical value like Union Pacific and Old Dominion to benefit from a cyclical discount. In other words, I take advantage of the time discount before earnings normalization. I would never ever use lower-quality companies for this thesis, as I want the timing of the cyclical recovery to be my main risk, not the survivability of my holdings.
  • I like a mix of upstream energy (producers) and midstream (pipelines) to benefit from energy normalization. The high equity yields provide investors with income, while others cannot wait for energy normalization.
  • My general dividend growth thesis is based on using time in the market for compounding purposes, something most funds cannot do due to the reasons mentioned in this article.

And, to go back to the article where I first discussed the Equity Yield Curve, I also noted that I had to carefully communicate my strategy in my articles. Murray Stahl was known for holding high-conviction positions through many cycles. It’s why he became a billionaire, as he stuck to high-conviction investments and didn’t take profits on things that kept working.

On Seeking Alpha, that is harder, as we all have different strategies, goals, and ages. I cannot possibly tell an 85-year-old to buy stock XYZ with a holding period of at least 20 years. That’s another reason why I use TOLL, as most asset-light compounders lack the income that many investors need. Midstream stocks, like EPD, may lack certain asset-light benefits, yet they provide the income many need and other TOLL benefits that make them suitable long-term investors.

Takeaway

Murray Stahl’s passing is a huge loss for the financial community, especially because many of his teachings seem to be more important than ever right now. As I have mentioned this so much, I could do it in my sleep, because we are dealing with countless challenges and structural changes that make it very likely that the next decade looks very different from the last.

That requires potential changes without the risks of us turning our portfolios into trading vehicles. After all, we want long-term compounding wealth, not actively managing our wealth.

Findings like the Equity Yield Curve can help us find long-term opportunities, while a focus on asset-light croupiers (or the TOLL model) provides support for asset selection.

It also brought me to my slogan:

“Time is the edge, quality is the filter, valuation is the trigger.”

In other words, I approach markets with a simple focus, which is to focus on durable compounders, buy them when they are unloved, and then let time in the market do the rest.

data.title: Alibaba: Burning Profits To Build China's AI Stack, And It's Working
data.description:
Introduction

It has been a while since I last reviewed Alibaba (BABA), and since the end of November, the company has lost around 19.2% despite my Strong Buy rating. In full transparency, I sold around 2/3 of my holdings in the firm at around $180 a share and sold the remainder at $140 to finance my current stake in JD.com (JD), as I believe that it might be a better opportunity considering its cash position, and I wanted to only have exposure to one Chinese firm. With that in mind, I’ll dive into what has been happening at Alibaba and see if this has changed the story.

Current Dynamics

Alibaba has been undergoing a massive investment cycle in AI, akin to hyperscalers, as CapEx surged massively for AI infrastructure. This has caused a 74% decrease in income from operations and a subsequent collapse in free cash flow. This has caused the market to punish the firm’s stock. Moreover, there was also the disposal of the Sun Art and Intime retail businesses, which has reduced the consolidated revenue growth figure. Though on an adjusted view, revenue actually grew by a strong 9%, telling us that the core digital businesses remain robust.

Despite that drop, Alibaba still has one of the best balance sheets in the worldwide tech sector, with around $80.1B in cash and liquid investments as of the last fiscal quarter. This allows the firm to sustain its heavy investment commitment to AI and cloud infrastructure. This will also be set to become a great AI stack akin to Google’s, that will integrate the firm’s own chip design with cloud infrastructure and LLMs.

Diving deeper, the Cloud Intelligence Group has emerged as the main piece of the firm’s growth plan as it has seen revenue grow by 36% on a Y/Y basis. This is set to support the core e-commerce segment, as AI-related workloads are now the main driver of new customer acquisition and increased spending from existing clients. The model-as-a-service framework has allowed BABA to monetize its Qwen family of models by charging for token consumption and associated computing power.

To support this and not let associated costs run wild, the cloud segment has deployed a set of proprietary tech designed to optimize resource utilization. For example, Aegaoun, which is a schedule and memory management technology that has achieved an 82% reduction in GPU resource requirements for inferencing workloads involving multiple models. By allowing a single GPU to run tens of models simultaneously and offloading data to host memory, BABA has thus bypassed physical memory limitations that previously led to thousands of idle GPUs.

BABA also shipped 470K AI chips that include the edge AI processors and high-performance cloud accelerators. Of these, the Zhenwu 810E GPU is mainly notable as it delivers performance relative to Nvidia’s H20. Alibaba also launched Wukong, its AI-native platform set specifically for the enterprise market, and unlike a personal assistant, Wukong is an orchestration layer that coordinates multiple specialized agents to handle complex multi-step workflows. This agentic shift is viewed by management as the key to expanding the TAM for cloud services. As these agents begin to handle mainstream work tasks, the proportion of corporate revenue spent on cloud and software is expected to expand by several multiples.

The core e-commerce businesses, which are Taobao and the Tmall Group, have been undergoing a period of stabilization as the domestic market has been fighting an intense competition with relatively diminishing returns. China's commerce has still grown by 6% in RMB terms, but margins have continued to be pressured by aggressive strategic investments. The branding of ele.me to Taobao Instant Commerce during the quarter was a pivotal move to align local services more closely with the flagship shopping app. This integration has seen significant traction, with the combined Taobao Instant and Ele.me platforms reportedly reaching 80MM daily orders by early 2026.

Valuation

BABA is still relatively cheap, especially thanks to its latest drawdown in stock price, as its P/E Non-GAAP is 23.35, which is around the middle of its peers, as Amazon (AMZN) is at 33.25 and Microsoft (MSFT) is at 24.11. The same could be said on an EV/EBITDA basis at 14.49, while on a free cash flow basis, the high investments in AI have depressed the yield to a relatively low 2.9%. The current dividend yield is also fairly low at 0.8%, while the buyback yield is at 0.2%. However, the price was also recently sustained by the bullish thesis of Michael Burry, who has continued his buying frenzy on Chinese stocks, mainly JD.com and Alibaba. I also did various analyses below to get a better idea of the underlying valuation of the firm, and these all have a potential upside.

I’ll say that BABA’s international platforms are increasingly focused on operational efficiency and regional scaling. For example, Trendyol is aggressively expanding in Europe, aiming for 4MM active shoppers in Romania by late 2026. Trendyol’s trajectory, which is majority-owned by Alibaba, has been a great success story as it transformed into Europe’s fastest-growing e-commerce ecosystem. The app is also highly popular, as it has been downloaded over 7MM times in the CEE region in 2024 alone. As the market for AI training data tightens, the firm’s cloud segment is also pivoting its infrastructure to prioritize the speed and relevance of data. As such, BABA is moving toward a unified platform, with the likes of Holgres, that can handle the entire lifecycle of data in a single environment.

Risks

Alibaba is facing cutthroat competition from domestic rivals in its core market, which has been eroding already slim margins due to the nature of the industry. BABA has lost market share to Pinduoduo (PDD) and Douyin, which have leveraged even lower price strategies. This has pushed BABA into an aggressive push for the quick commerce sector that has triggered an intense subsidy war. Meaning that the segment remains highly unprofitable, with targets for positive cash flow not expected until fiscal year 2028.

The shift toward a tech-first architecture also requires massive investments. The aggressive spending on AI infrastructure and data center expansion has led to dramatic profit declines, with the 67% drop in net income in fiscal Q3. Plus, there is clear uncertainty about the monetization potential of these AI investments into recurring revenue.

Conclusion

Alibaba has passed through an immense structural transition, as reflected by the firm’s stock price. It has accepted the end of the hypergrowth e-commerce era and is pivoting decisively toward a future defined by AI agents and cloud infrastructure. Though the underlying numbers have been scary, I believe that the company can make the shift into a leading tech infrastructure play in China. I rate Alibaba as a buy.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Flight Cuts Deepen On Fuel Shock
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Airlines cut global schedules as Iran-driven energy shock ripples into May. (00:14) Tesla wins first European approval for FSD supervised. (01:54) GSK bets big on cancer comeback with aggressive trial push. (02:52)

This is an abridged transcript.

Airlines are scaling back flight schedules more aggressively as disruptions tied to the Iran conflict continue to constrain energy flows, according to new research from BNP Paribas.

Analyst Matt Akers said in a note to clients on Friday that carriers have reduced global capacity for April by roughly 7%, a deeper pullback than the 5% cut estimated just a week earlier.

The schedules for May are also starting to weaken, now running about 2% below pre-conflict levels, compared with a marginal decline previously.

The data suggests that the impact on air travel is becoming more pronounced as elevated fuel costs and operational uncertainty weigh on airline planning.

The Middle East remains the epicenter of the disruption. Flight schedules in the region are down sharply, with April capacity off by about 43% compared with pre-conflict plans, and May schedules currently trailing by around 14%.

Outside the Middle East, reductions are more moderate but still broadening. Asian carriers have trimmed April schedules by nearly 5% and May by just under 1%, while European airlines are down about 4% for April and slightly below 1% for May. In North America, cuts have been relatively limited, with capacity reductions of roughly 2% for both months.

Akers noted that if fuel prices remain elevated, airlines may begin accelerating the retirement of older, less efficient aircraft.

For now, demand appears resilient. Recent checks with airlines in late March indicated little change in passenger behavior, even as geopolitical tensions intensified and operating costs climbed.

Dutch regulators have cleared the use of Tesla’s (TSLA) self-driving software with mandatory human supervision on highways and city streets.

This marks a European first for the EV maker as it pushes for broader adoption across the European Union.

The Netherlands’ approval of the system, branded Full Self-Driving Supervised, which can steer, brake, and accelerate, follows more than 18 months of testing and evaluation by the Dutch vehicle authority RDW.

RDW noted that EU vehicle approval standards impose stricter safety requirements than those in the U.S.

It said, "This means that the FSD Supervised version in the U.S. is NOT comparable to the FSD Supervised version in the EU," but did not provide further details.

Tesla’s (TSLA) European sales showed tentative signs of recovery, rising in February for the first time in over a year.

GSK (GSK) is preparing a high-stakes push into oncology.

The company is lining up five late-stage trials at once for a promising cancer drug, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

The British drugmaker, long criticized for a weak pipeline, is attempting to accelerate development of mocertatug rezetecan, or Mo-Rez, with three trials in ovarian cancer and two in endometrial cancer.

Early results for Mo-Rez are encouraging. Data presented at a recent oncology conference showed response rates of 62% in ovarian cancer patients resistant to certain chemotherapy and 67% in endometrial cancer patients, meaning tumors shrank or disappeared in those cases.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Chinese rare earth producers hike prices by 44% for Q2

‘Annoyance Economy’ costs U.S. households $165B a year, study finds

Wall Street trading desks poised for $40B quarter amid geopolitical turmoil

Catalyst watch:

  • The four-day Space Symposium will begin. The broad space industry conference that brings together leaders from government, defense, commercial space, and research organizations. Northrop Grumman (NOC) and Lockheed Martin (LMT) are two of the notable companies participating. The symposium has added significance after the Artemis mission and anticipated increase in defense-related spending.

  • The four-day CinemaCon event in Las Vegas begins. Studios with presentations scheduled include Sony Pictures (SONY), Warner Bros. (WBD), Universal Pictures (CMCSA), Amazon MGM Studios (AMZN), Paramount Pictures (PSKY), and Walt Disney Studios (DIS).

Stock index futures are down after U.S.-Iran talks collapsed and Washington announced it will begin a blockade of Iranian ports.

U.S. Central Command posted Sunday that it will begin enforcing a maritime blockade on all traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports starting Monday, April 13, at 10 a.m. ET, following a presidential directive.

British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said that, no matter the pressure, Britain would not be drawn into the Iran war or take part in any blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Crude oil is up 8% at $104. Bitcoin is little changed at $70,000 and Gold is down 0.8% at $4,714.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.4% and the DAX is down 1%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Children’s Place (PLCE) -16% - Shares tumbled after a weak Q4 marked by a sharp earnings and revenue miss, with sales down 19.4% Y/Y and comparable sales falling 10.7%, driven by weaker e-commerce traffic, reduced Amazon shipments, and soft consumer demand.

Economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Existing Home Sales

data.title: Getting Smarter - The Case For Global GDP Weighted Sov.Bond Indexes Strengthens?
data.description:
By Robin Marshall, Head, FICC Research

  • The case for introducing fundamentally-driven, or smart, global bond market indexes has been reinforced by both rapid growth in sovereign bond issuance and DM debt levels in recent years, and faster relative economic growth in EM economies.
  • This is highlighted by outperformance of these smart indexes, relative to pure market value versions, judged by mean-variance metrics, i.e., the debt capacity version of the WGBI, and GDP-weighted versions of the WGBI.
  • GDP-weighted indexes help capture the impact of faster EM growth, and changes in the structure of the global economy. They also protect passive investors from the automatic increase in weightings in more indebted DM sovereigns that can occur, and particularly during a debt crisis.
  • The Eurozone sovereign debt crisis showed these potential risks in pure market value driven indexes while the GFC showed similar risks in corporate bond indexes dominated by bank bonds.
  • Both nominal GDP and PPP-based GDP country weight variants of the FTSE Russell WGBI outperformed the original WGBI since 2015.
  • PPP-based GDP weights are the purest form of GDP country weighting, even if they are reported with a lag and have some measurement issues. They are more stable than nominal GDP-weighted measures, and less exposed to large exchange rate swings.
  • GDP country weighting has less impact on EM indexes, since China already has a substantial weighting, and it modestly reduces historical returns, though it does create a more balanced EM profile and reduces China concentration risk.
  • Country weighting shows how standard indices can be customised to capture changes in the structure of the global economy and fixed income markets.

In a previous paper1, we examined country weights in global govt bond indexes and the performance of the FTSE Russell Debt Capacity World Govt Bond index (DCWGBI) after 10 years. The DCWGBI adjusts country weights for a country’s debt capacity, avoiding "debtor bias", whereas the original FTSE Russell WGBI, first introduced 40 yrs ago, has country weights based solely on the size of the country’s debt market value and exchange rate movements.

Thus the US, for example, has a 15% higher weighting in the WGBI than the DCWGBI. In addition, we found that the DCWGBI had outperformed the WGBI in its first 10 yrs, reflecting investor disquiet with more highly indebted sovereigns. The DCWGBI, as an alternative global benchmark to the original WGBI, with a clear country weighting methodology, enables investors to incorporate debt capacity considerations in their investment decisions, should they wish to do so.

Focus on growth and sov.debt levels shows how "smart" benchmarks can work

In recent years, as global debt levels of major economies have increased, and focus on fundamental factors has increased, the case for introducing fundamentally driven or "smart" benchmarks, like GDP-weighted government bond indexes, as an alternative to market value (MV) debt-weighted indexes has strengthened. The Eurozone sovereign debt crisis was a good example of the inherent risks in MV debt-weighted benchmarks, partly due to contagion. Even before the Eurozone debt crisis, Bruder2 et al found evidence of outperformance by GDP weighted benchmarks versus MV debt-weighted indexes.

The difficulty here is that as debt levels rise in MV indexes, the weight of more indebted countries automatically increases (the so-called "bums problem" in fixed income indexing). So, a passive investor in a bond market index based on the MV of debt alone is not taking a neutral view, since the investor is increasing exposure to more indebted countries as the issuance increases (or corporates in a corporate bond index). There is also evidence from other asset classes of benchmarks based on MV alone causing global index weightings to be distorted, i.e., Japan’s weight in global equity indexes in the late 1980s.

GDP growth and scale important measures of debt capacity…

There are a number of factors supporting GDP-weighted indices fundamentally. Firstly, GDP growth and GDP size is an important measure of debt capacity. Thus, sustainability metrics like debt levels, or debt service costs are not based on outright levels alone but as ratios of debt to GDP. Ceteris paribus, faster growing economies therefore are less exposed to debt sustainability issues, when the denominator rises faster in the ratio of debt/GDP. This should lower sovereign default risks. In contrast, Chart 1 shows the rapid growth in G7 debt/GDP ratios since the GFC, raising investor debt sustainability concerns.

GDP weights recognise changes in the world economy & reduce concentration risk

Secondly, GDP weighting for bond indices recognises the impact of major shifts in the structure of the global economy in recent years, particularly faster relative GDP growth in emerging markets, alongside slower G7 growth. In that regard, we note EM local currency government bond markets have delivered far stronger total returns than G7 markets since Covid, as Chart 2 shows.

Thirdly, using GDP weights increases the diversification of global bond indices, and reduces concentration risk in a smaller number of highly indebted G7 nations. It also dampens the effect of a strong or weak dollar in raising, or reducing the US country weight, and stabilises country weights. The combination of the strong US dollar from 2014-24 and rapid increase in US debt issuance took the US weight in the WGBI up to 45% at the peak, and it still exceeds 40%.

Nominal GDP versus PPP GDP weights in government bond indices

Building global bond indices based on GDP weights raises a further question – whether to use nominal GDP or Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) GDP weights in global GDP? Nominal GDP weights use prevailing market exchange rates and GDP levels and then calculate the share of that country’s GDP in global GDP, in a common numeraire, normally the US dollar. This measure gives a snapshot of the nominal value of a country’s GDP, relative size and ability to import goods. However, nominal GDP makes no allowance for differences in the cost of living in different economies, and is sensitive to movements in exchange rates. So US dollar appreciation from 2014-24 increased the nominal value of US GDP relative to other economies, and the US weight in global GDP.

PPP exchange rates allow for non-tradeable goods and are more stable…

To make allowance for differences in the cost of living, the PPP exchange rate can be used. The PPP exchange rate is the conversion rate between the currency of one country and another that equalises the cost of the same basket of goods in the two countries. A frequently quoted example is the so-called Big-Mac3 index, in which the cost of hamburgers is compared in different countries, giving an implied PPP exchange rate between countries for hamburgers.

To enable broader price comparisons across countries, the International Comparisons Program was implemented by the UN and University of Pennsylvania in 1968, and PPP exchange rates are based on this global price survey. PPP exchange rates tend to be more stable than market exchange rates over time, and capture the impact of non-tradeable goods and services on economic well-being. Market exchange rates are only relevant for internationally traded goods.

…biggest differences between market & PPP exchange rates are for EM economies

For EM economies, the gap between market and PPP exchange rates is generally much larger than for advanced economies. Indeed, for most EM economies, the ratio of market and PPP US dollar exchange rates is between 2 and 4. So using PPP exchange rates for EM economies boosts their global GDP weights considerably. The OECD uses PPP exchange rates to calculate global GDP weights, while the World Bank and IMF use both PPP and market exchange rates. The natural time lag between the price surveys and publication of the results is an obvious deficiency of PPP exchange rates and neither methodology is perfect, which explains why the IMF, for example, uses both methodologies. But PPP exchange rates may be a better guide to overall living standards, even if there are measurement issues and they cannot be updated as frequently as market exchange rates.

Performance & underlying characteristics of GDP-weighted govt bond indexes

Turning to the performance and characteristics of global GDP country weights, the FTSE Russell (PPP) GDP country weights, based on IMF data, show EM economy weights are greater on a PPP basis, than their WGBI weights, and particularly China, which has a 33.7% weight. In comparison, (PPP) GDP country weights of DM economies are mainly below WGBI weights, due to higher issuance in those DM countries. The US (PPP) GDP weighting is below that of China, at 25.6%. Table 1 shows these weighting differences.

GDP-weighting methodology adjustment has less impact on EMGBI

Extending the GDP-weighting methodology to the Emerging Markets Government Bond index (EMGBI) makes less difference to overall performance, but does increase the country weights of India and Brazil in the index notably, by 12% and 4%, whilst reducing China’s country weight by 13.5%. This causes the GDP-weighted variants of the EMGBI to underperform the EMGBI modestly, as Table 3 shows, since China government debt has outperformed virtually all govt bond markets over the period since 2015, given the combination of deflation risks and the safe haven appeal of government bonds during the property crash.

…but reduces concentration risk in China

However, GDP-weighting the EMGBI gives a better diversified index by country weight, and reduces concentration risk in China, where yields are now near all-time lows. So the China effect within the EMGBI index from using GDP-weights works the other way from the WGBI-GDP adjusted variants, with China’s weighting falling to 45% from 58% in the EMGBI index.

Country-weights show how standard indices can be flexed for structural changes

Finally, we would point out that both the WGBI and EMGBÍ country-weighted indices are simply examples of how standard, market-value driven indices can be customised, or flexed, for a new global fixed income regime of more indebted sovereigns and faster growing EM nations. These variants have the attraction of protecting investors from concentration risk and the debtor bias in pure market-value indices, and increase the weighting of faster growing economies with more debt service capacity. Methodologically, this seems a good starting point. But these are not the only customisations available – FTSE Russell already has a China-capped EMGBI version which reduces China concentration risk further, and we continue research on factor-tilted variations, to capture structural change in the global economy and fixed income markets.

data.title: Shake Shack: Growth Setup Has Improved (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Shake Shack Inc. (SHAK) previously with a hold rating, as I thought it was too early to be bullish given the issues in Northeast markets and pricing pressure. My view has changed. Traffic has now improved for two straight quarters, the quality of comparable sales growth looks better, and store execution is clearly getting stronger. With build costs coming down and SHAK adding more tools to drive frequency, I now think the bull case is more attractive.

The traffic recovery now looks real

The weakness in traffic was one of the biggest reasons why I gave a hold rating on SHAK, but this aspect of the story has gotten better. SHAK has managed to print two consecutive quarters of positive traffic growth (the latest, Q4, saw 50 bps traffic growth), and this matters more than the headline comparable sales growth figure because it implies that SHAK is no longer just relying on price to grow. The trend behind this improvement is also very encouraging, as comparable sales actually improved sequentially each month, and this was despite the last six weeks getting hit by bad weather in the Northeast. Even in January, comparable sales came in at 4.3% despite a ~400 bps weather headwind (this is in line with Bloomberg's alternative tracking data).

The recovery trend is also becoming visible across all regions. SHAK delivered positive comparable sales in nearly all regions: the West was strongest at 5%, the South and Midwest were both 2%, and the Northeast was 1%. NYC, including Manhattan, was still negative 2%, but I think the setup has still improved. SHAK is no longer just relying on this important market (within the Northeast region) to grow; other regions are also helping to drive growth in the business.

I don't think this traffic recovery and regional performance were due to luck. I believe it was due to SHAK doing a solid job executing better in stores. Recall previously that SHAK showed better labor and margin performance. For the former, as a reference, labor as a percent of sales was 25.4% in Q4, down 150 bps y/y. That is obviously good for margins, but the takeaway for readers here is how SHAK achieved this. This is important because it gives confidence that it is a structural move. SHAK has managed to shift more stores to operate within its target labor model (improved from ~50% in mid-2024 to consistently >90% in 2025), and the direct impact was apparent. Wait times improved from ~7 minutes in 2023 to <6 minutes in 2025, which should support a better guest experience and reduce friction at the store level. Team member tenure also increased ~40% since 2023, which suggests lower turnover and a more experienced workforce. Longer-term, with a stronger labor force and better-trained teams, it should translate to more consistent execution, better throughput, and fewer operational mistakes.

The next leg of growth

The forward growth outlook has also gotten easier to underwrite. Firstly, the return on investment [ROI] hurdle has gotten lower. The average net build cost for new stores is now reduced to less than $2 million in 2025, which is down ~20% y/y. Lifetime pre-opening expense for the 2025 class also fell ~14% vs. the 2024 class. This means that SHAK can now consider more locations that would have failed the ROI test previously. The fact that SHAK opened 45 company-operated stores in 2025, the largest class to date, does support my view on this.

Secondly, which is the bigger development, is that SHAK now has more than one way to drive frequency? Specifically, SHAK launched the We Really Cook campaign, whose aim is to build better top-of-funnel awareness around food quality. Within the campaign, it includes the $1, $3, and $5 app-only promotions, which have helped to drive app downloads up by ~50% since launch. This shouldn't be viewed as something like giving free discounts. By doing it via the app, SHAK is able to convert app users to frequent users (which is validated by the fact that app traffic is up >50%), which would give SHAK a direct channel to bring guests back and engage them more consistently over time. At the same time, this allows SHAK to protect the premium brand position (note: this was a concern of mine previously).

Valuation

I believe consensus has started to price in these improvements, as you can see from the EBITDA expectations, but the broader market is apparently not giving SHAK any credit for the improvements it has delivered. The forward EBITDA multiple fell sharply to just 16x from 23x previously, despite the fact that traffic has really inflected, comparable sales quality growth improved (not reliant on pricing now), and in-store operations have evidently gotten better.

We don't need to make aggressive assumptions to make attractive returns at this valuation. Management implicitly guided for a 15% adj. EBITDA growth, and if we extrapolate that, which shouldn't be tough given the comparable sales trend, enlarged locations' TAM, and better labor efficiency (helps with margin expansion), SHAK should print more than $300 million adj. EBITDA in FY2028. The current multiple of 16x forward EBITDA is below SHAK's 10-year -1 standard deviation, and I am expecting that to re-rate back to ~20x, as this is the past two years' average (reflects SHAK's valuation before the recent de-rating).

Risk

Some parts of the growth story now rely on SHAK leaning more on its app and loyalty program strategy. Given that these tools are still in the relatively early stage, they may not scale as smoothly as I hoped. Beef inflation also remains a real cost headwind, and if that surges, SHAK could still see margin pressure.

Conclusion

I am upgrading SHAK to a buy rating. Not only did traffic improve, but the growth setup also looks more durable now. Growth is less reliant on pricing, in-store operations have structurally improved, and lower build costs should enlarge new units' growth potential. On top of that, SHAK now has more than one way to drive repeat visits. At the current valuation, I think the upside now outweighs the risks.

data.title: FCOM: An Overlooked Dividend Growth Opportunity With Big Tech Exposure
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Fidelity MSCI Communications Services Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FCOM) deserves a buy rating due to the fund’s hybrid growth and income opportunities that are not yet fully appreciated by the market. While many of FCOM’s top holdings are tech companies that have generated significant capital appreciation, another overlooked quality is its dividend growth potential. History has shown that top dividend growers have delivered strong risk-adjusted returns particularly compared to companies that offer no dividends, making FCOM relatively undervalued.

Fidelity MSCI Communications Services Index ETF – Overview and Compared ETFs

FCOM is a passive ETF that tracks an index consisting of U.S. communications companies including media, interactive gaming, and advertising. With its inception in 2013, the fund has 92 holdings and $1.69B in AUM. The fund is cap-weighted and uses a representative sampling strategy. I wrote previously on the fund last year and still rate the ETF a buy for the reasons discussed in this article.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are State Street Communication Services Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLC), Vanguard Communication Services ETF (VOX), and iShares Global Comm Services ETF (IXP). XLC aims to capture comm sector holdings within the S&P 500 Index. VOX is a passive fund that also captures the comm sector and is the heaviest on interactive media, entertainment, and integrated telecommunications. IXP is included for comparison because it captures a global view of communication services and captures large, mid, and small cap companies.

FCOM: Performance, Expense Ratio, and Dividend Yield

FCOM has seen a 10-year average annual return of 10.64%. The Fidelity fund has outperformed both VOX and IXP, which had 10-year average returns of 8.54% and 8.82%, respectively. XLC has seen an average annual return of 11.84% since its 2018 inception. All funds underperformed the S&P 500 Index over the past 10 years. This underperformance is predominantly because high-growth companies like Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL), (GOOG) were not added to the communications sector until 2018.

While FCOM has experienced strong capital appreciation, I believe an underappreciated quality is the fund’s dividend growth. FCOM has seen a 3-year dividend growth CAGR of over 25%. Although the fund has only a 0.95% yield currently, FCOM’s top holdings have significant dividend growth capacity which will continue to fuel dividend growth in coming years. The fund also has a very affordable 0.08% expense ratio.

FCOM Holdings: Strengths and Outlook Factors

FCOM’s top holdings include META, GOOGL, GOOG, Netflix (NFLX), and Verizon (VZ). This composition is starkly different from a decade ago when the sector was dominated by telecom and traditional media instead of its current mega-cap holdings. Other funds have a similar mix of holdings. However, XLC includes Live Nation Entertainment (LYV) and IXP, the only global comm fund, also includes Tencent Holdings (TCEHY).

Almost 50% of FCOM’s weight is in Meta Platforms, Alphabet, and Netflix. While this presents concentration risk, I believe Meta and Google will significantly grow their dividends in coming years. Additionally, I believe Netflix has long-term optionality to initiate a dividend, increasing the attractiveness of each of FCOM’s top-weighted holdings.

Meta Platforms: Offering a Blend between Potential Income and Growth

You may not think of Meta Platforms as a significant dividend provider. However, I believe the company is positioned for stable, dividend growth in coming years. A key factor for this claim is Meta’s daily active user count which reached a record 3.58B at the end of last year. More importantly, Meta’s average revenue per person reached $16.56 in Q4 2025, representing another record. This steady growth, along with the company’s low dividend payout ratio of just 7.1%, puts Meta Platforms in a prime position for dividend growth. The company announced its first-ever dividend back in February 2024 and its forward dividend yield currently stands at just 0.33%. However, given its consistent revenue, stable user count, and low payout ratio, I believe Meta is prime to increase its dividend yield in the coming years.

Capital expenditure for Meta also saw a huge increase between 2024 and 2025, reaching $72.2B in 2025, up from $39.2B. This capex is about to be translated into products including Meta Quest 4, expected to be released later this year. A key feature for the Quest 4 is eye tracking, which puts the product on par with the Apple Vision Pro, costing over $3,000 compared to the Meta Quest 4 at only $800. Therefore, Meta will continue to hold a competitive advantage in the virtual reality market, offering investors a strong blend of capital appreciation and income potential.

Alphabet: Significant Subscriptions Growth will Drive a Higher Dividend Yield

The second key strength for FCOM is its weight in Google. The largest growth segments for Alphabet have been Google Search and subscription services as both segments saw 17% YoY growth. These segments have translated into a steady source of revenue for the company, and 22% YoY increase in operating income. Google also saw 750 million Gemini App monthly active users and 325 million paid consumer subscriptions across platforms like YouTube.

2026 will be a key year for Google as it looks to potentially double its capital expenditure on AI-driven infrastructure like data centers and servers. This spending will ensure that Google remains on the leading edge of search monetization as other competitors like Microsoft (MSFT) are also seeing heavy AI investment. Even with competitive pressures, Google’s net income margin of 32.8% and dividend payout ratio of just 7% mean that the company has significant dividend growth potential. Although its current dividend yield stands at just 0.26%, I believe Alphabet’s steady revenue growth from subscriptions and search will make it a prime dividend grower over the next decade.

Netflix: Strong Consumer Loyalty and Content Investment will Perpetuate FCF Growth

A key challenge for companies like Netflix stems from consumer loyalty. This is because steady revenue for companies like Netflix is reliant on customers and viewers that stick with these platforms. However, digital service companies reported low consumer loyalty so far in 2026. Loyalty programs and incentives showed only limited results. Thankfully, for companies like Netflix, subscribers are relatively exclusive with 80% not also subscribing to competitor services.

However, this loyalty relies on consistent quality products and content. Like Meta and Google, Netflix has the capacity for both growth and dividend initiation. For example, its spending on upcoming content hit a record high last year, totaling $18B, compared with $16.2B in the previous year. The company’s 16% YoY revenue growth and 24% net income margin have translated to a 17% YoY levered FCF growth. Therefore, while Netflix's leadership has made no plans to initiate a dividend in the near term, I believe the company has both the competitive advantages and free cash flow to initiate a dividend within the next decade.

Why Dividend Growth Matters

So, why should investors care about dividend growth? In a 40-year study, companies within the S&P 500 Index that grew their dividends saw higher total performance compared to those companies that did not pay or did not grow their dividends. FCOM’s top holdings represent companies that demonstrate strong dividend growth and therefore historically outperform non-dividend payers.

As we have seen in the past, FCOM’s top holdings could change. High-growth companies with little to no dividend yield could replace holdings like Meta and Google. However, I believe these companies will maintain dominant positions in the coming years and investors will benefit significantly from their dividend growth.

Risks to Investors

Aside from the concentration risk previously discussed, another key risk to my thesis and for investors is the impact of long-term dividend growth at the expense of capital appreciation. Verizon, FCOM’s #5 holding at 4.8% weight, has increased its dividend for 20 consecutive years. In doing so, the fund has a current yield of 5.9% with a dividend payout ratio of 58%. As a result, the company has less capacity for capital expenditure and growth. Consequently, revenue growth has been relatively flat for the past five quarters.

Should FCOM’s top holdings expand their dividends too quickly, the fund risks low growth at the expense of income generation. Therefore, while dividend growth is an important quality, too much of something can still be a bad thing. Investors seeking purely dividends are likely to be disappointed in overall returns due to traditionally lower capital appreciation at the expense of a high dividend yield.

Current Valuation and Outlook

FCOM has outperformed most peer funds and the S&P 500 Index over the past year. A key driving factor is Google’s recent performance including a 107% one-year share price return. This is predominantly due to the shifting view of Google which was previously thought to be threatened by ChatGPT.

Despite FCOM’s strong recent performance, the ETF still has a favorable valuation compared to the rest of the U.S. market. For example, the P/E ratio for the S&P 500 Index stands at nearly 30x. Due to FCOM’s current valuation and its blend of capital appreciation and dividend growth potential, I believe FCOM will outperform both its long-term average and the S&P 500 over the next couple years.
